# Colophon registry initialization

zrbz_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBZ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbz already kindled"

  # Verify buz zipper is kindled (CLI furnish must kindle buz first)
  zbuz_sentinel

  # Accounts — Google Cloud service accounts (rbw-a)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_ACCOUNTS   "rbw-a"   "Accounts — Service account management"
  local z_mod="rbgp_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_MANTLE_GOVERNOR         "rbw-aM"  "${z_mod}" "rbgp_governor_mantle"       ""        "Create/replace governor service account"
  z_mod="rbgg_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_INVEST_RETRIEVER        "rbw-arI" "${z_mod}" "rbgg_invest_retriever"      "param1"  "Invest a Retriever service account for an identity"
  buz_enroll RBZ_INVEST_DIRECTOR         "rbw-adI" "${z_mod}" "rbgg_invest_director"       "param1"  "Invest a Director service account for an identity"
  buz_enroll RBZ_DIVEST_RETRIEVER        "rbw-arD" "${z_mod}" "rbgg_divest_retriever"      "param1"  "Divest a Retriever service account by identity"
  buz_enroll RBZ_DIVEST_DIRECTOR         "rbw-adD" "${z_mod}" "rbgg_divest_director"       "param1"  "Divest a Director service account by identity"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ROSTER_RETRIEVERS       "rbw-arr" "${z_mod}" "rbgg_roster_retrievers"     ""        "Roster Retriever service accounts (emit per-identity fact files)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ROSTER_DIRECTORS        "rbw-adr" "${z_mod}" "rbgg_roster_directors"      ""        "Roster Director service accounts (emit per-identity fact files)"

  # Access — credential access probes (rbw-ac), one colophon per role
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_ACCESS     "rbw-ac"  "Access — Credential access probes"
  z_mod="rbgv_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CHECK_GOVERNOR          "rbw-acg" "${z_mod}" "rbgv_check_governor"        ""        "Check the governor credential reaches Google Cloud (JWT SA access probe)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CHECK_RETRIEVER         "rbw-acr" "${z_mod}" "rbgv_check_retriever"       ""        "Check the retriever credential reaches Google Cloud (JWT SA access probe)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CHECK_DIRECTOR          "rbw-acd" "${z_mod}" "rbgv_check_director"        ""        "Check the director credential reaches Google Cloud (JWT SA access probe)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CHECK_PAYOR             "rbw-acp" "${z_mod}" "rbgv_check_payor"           ""        "Check the payor credential reaches Google Cloud (OAuth access probe)"

  # Crucible — container runtime (rbw-c)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_CRUCIBLE   "rbw-c"   "Crucible — Container runtime"
  z_mod="rbob_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_CHARGE  "rbw-cC"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_charge"       "imprint"  "Charge crucible (sentry + pentacle + bottle containers)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_QUENCH  "rbw-cQ"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_quench"       "imprint"  "Quench crucible"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_SSH     "rbw-cS"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_ssh"          "imprint"  "SSH into the bottle container"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_HAIL    "rbw-ch"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_hail"         "param1"   "Shell into the sentry container"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_RACK    "rbw-cr"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_rack"         "param1"   "Shell into the bottle container"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_SCRY    "rbw-cs"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_scry"         "param1"   "Observe network traffic on crucible containers"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_WRIT    "rbw-cw"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_writ"         "imprint"  "Non-interactive exec in sentry container"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_FIAT    "rbw-cf"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_fiat"         "imprint"  "Non-interactive exec in pentacle container"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_BARK    "rbw-cb"  "${z_mod}" "rbob_bark"         "imprint"  "Non-interactive exec in bottle container"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_ACTIVE  "rbw-cic" "${z_mod}" "rbob_charged"      "param1"   "Check if crucible is currently charged"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_KLUDGE_BOTTLE "rbw-cKB" "${z_mod}" "rbob_kludge_bottle" "param1" "Kludge bottle vessel and drive hallmark into nameplate"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CRUCIBLE_KLUDGE_SENTRY "rbw-cKS" "${z_mod}" "rbob_kludge_sentry" "param1" "Kludge sentry vessel and drive hallmark into nameplate"

  # Depot — GCP project infrastructure (rbw-d, UPPER=mutates, lower=read)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_DEPOT      "rbw-d"   "Depot — GCP project infrastructure"
  z_mod="rbgp_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_LEVY_DEPOT             "rbw-dL"  "${z_mod}" "rbgp_depot_levy"   ""  "Provision GCP depot project"
  buz_enroll RBZ_UNMAKE_DEPOT           "rbw-dU"  "${z_mod}" "rbgp_depot_unmake"  "param1"  "Permanently remove a depot (operator supplies depot project ID)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_LIST_DEPOT             "rbw-dl"  "${z_mod}" "rbgp_depot_list"    ""  "List all active depots"
  buz_enroll RBZ_INFO_DEPOT             "rbw-di"  "${z_mod}" "rbgp_depot_info"    ""  "Run egress posture checks against the live depot's worker pools"
  z_mod="rbfl0_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_YOKE_RELIQUARY         "rbw-dY"  "${z_mod}" "rbfl_yoke"          "param1"  "Yoke a reliquary touchmark into every vessel's rbrv.env (wildcard fan-out)"

  # Lode — fetched-side universal capture (rbw-l, UPPER=mutates GAR/cost, lower=read-only)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_LODE       "rbw-l"   "Lode — Fetched-side universal capture"
  z_mod="rbld0_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ENSCONCE_BOLE          "rbw-lE"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_ensconce"      "param1"  "Ensconce an upstream base image into a Lode (capture)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_CONCLAVE_RELIQUARY     "rbw-lC"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_conclave"      ""        "Conclave the build-tool cohort into one Lode (capture)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_UNDERPIN_WSL           "rbw-lU"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_underpin"      "param1"  "Underpin a vendor WSL rootfs into a Lode (capture; args: release point)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_IMMURE_PODVM           "rbw-lI"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_immure"        "param1"  "Immure podman-machine disk leaves of one quay family into a Lode (capture; args: family version)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_DIVINE_LODES           "rbw-ld"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_divine"        ""        "Divine Lodes — enumerate every Lode by touchmark (read-only)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_AUGUR_LODE             "rbw-la"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_augur"         "param1"  "Augur a Lode — inspect member tags and decode its rbi_vouch envelope (read-only)"
  buz_enroll RBZ_BANISH_LODE            "rbw-lB"  "${z_mod}" "rbld_banish"        "param1"  "Banish a Lode — delete the whole rbi_ld/<touchmark> package"

  # Guide — human-directed procedures (rbw-g)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_GUIDE      "rbw-g"   "Guide — Human-directed procedures"
  z_mod="rbgp_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_PAYOR_INSTALL          "rbw-gPI" "${z_mod}" "rbgp_payor_install"   "param1"  "Ingest OAuth credentials from JSON key file"
  z_mod="rbh0/rbhp0_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_PAYOR_ESTABLISH        "rbw-gPE" "${z_mod}" "rbhp_establish"       ""  "Guided Manor establishment — GCP project + OAuth consent screen"
  buz_enroll RBZ_PAYOR_REFRESH          "rbw-gPR" "${z_mod}" "rbhp_refresh"         ""  "Refresh expired OAuth tokens"
  buz_enroll RBZ_QUOTA_BUILD            "rbw-gq"  "${z_mod}" "rbhp_quota_build"     ""  "Display Cloud Build capacity review procedure"
  # Onboarding — handbook tracks (rbw-o terminal + rbw-O* family, see ₣A6 paddock)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_ONBOARDING "rbw-o"   "Onboarding — Handbook restart"
  z_mod="rbh0/rbho0_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_START_HERE    "rbw-o"   "${z_mod}" "rbho_start_here"            ""  "Onboarding start — probe-aware menu into handbook tracks"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_CRASH_COURSE  "rbw-Occ" "${z_mod}" "rbho_crash_course"          ""  "Crash Course — universal prerequisite: tabtargets, regimes, diagnostic failure"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_CRED_RETRIEVER "rbw-Ocr" "${z_mod}" "rbho_credential_retriever" ""  "Install retriever credentials — place RBRA key file"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_CRED_DIRECTOR  "rbw-Ocd" "${z_mod}" "rbho_credential_director"  ""  "Install director credentials — place RBRA key file"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_FIRST_CRUCIBLE "rbw-Ofc" "${z_mod}" "rbho_first_crucible"       ""  "Start a Crucible using local builds — kludge, charge, rack"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_TADMOR_SECURITY "rbw-Ots" "${z_mod}" "rbho_tadmor_security"     ""  "Verify Crucible containment under attack — charge tadmor and run the adversarial suite"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_DIR_FIRST_BUILD "rbw-Odf" "${z_mod}" "rbho_director_first_build" "" "Your First Cloud Build — inscribe, conjure, tour, summon, abjure"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_DIR_AIRGAP     "rbw-Oda" "${z_mod}" "rbho_director_airgap"      ""  "Airgap Cloud Build — ensconce, conjure base, conjure airgap, charge moriah, compare plumb"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_DIR_BIND       "rbw-Odb" "${z_mod}" "rbho_director_bind"        ""  "Bind Cloud Build — pin upstream image by digest, mode-mixture pluml Crucible"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_DIR_GRAFT      "rbw-Odg" "${z_mod}" "rbho_director_graft"       ""  "Graft Cloud Build — push locally-built image, inspect GRAFTED Vouch verdict"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_PAYOR_HB      "rbw-Op"  "${z_mod}" "rbho_payor_handbook"       ""  "Payor — establish a Manor and provision the Depot"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ONBOARD_GOVERNOR_HB   "rbw-Og"  "${z_mod}" "rbho_governor_handbook"    ""  "Governor — administer service accounts for directors and retrievers"

  # Foundry — registry artifact lifecycle (rbw-f, UPPER=mutates GAR, lower=read/local)
  buz_group RBZ__GROUP_FOUNDRY    "rbw-f"   "Foundry — Registry artifact lifecycle"
  z_mod="rbfd_cli.sh"
  buz_enroll RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK        "rbw-fO"  "${z_mod}" "rbfd_ordain"          "param1"  "Ordain hallmark: conjure, bind, or graft based on vessel mode"

content.text: Zipper shape is clear — `buz_enroll` carries name, colophon, module, function, channel, description. Now the enrollment primitive, the credential path, and how theurge dispatches.

input.description: Read buz_enroll function signature

content.content:
102:# buz_enroll() - Register colophon tuple in parallel rolls
103-# Args: varname, colophon, module, command, channel, description
104-# All 6 arguments required. Channel: "" (none), "imprint", or "param1".
105-# Assigns colophon string to caller's variable via printf -v
106-# Side effects: populates registry rolls (must be called in same process, NOT inside $())
107:buz_enroll() {
108-  zbuz_sentinel
109-
110-  test $# -eq 6 || buc_die "buz_enroll: requires 6 arguments (varname colophon module command channel description), got $#"
111-
112-  local -r z_varname="${1}"
113-  local -r z_colophon="${2}"
114-  local -r z_module="${3}"
115-  local -r z_command="${4}"
116-  local -r z_channel="${5}"
117-  local -r z_description="${6}"
118-  test -n "${z_varname}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: varname required"
119-  test -n "${z_colophon}"    || buc_die "buz_enroll: colophon required"
120-  test -n "${z_module}"      || buc_die "buz_enroll: module required"
121-  test -n "${z_command}"     || buc_die "buz_enroll: command required"
122-  test -n "${z_description}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: description required"
123-
124-  # Validate channel value (empty string is valid — means no channel)
125-  case "${z_channel}" in
126-    ""|"imprint"|"param1") ;;
127-    *) buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid channel: ${z_channel}" ;;
128-  esac
129-
130-  # Validate variable name
131-  [[ "${z_varname}" =~ ^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$ ]] \
132-    || buc_die "buz_enroll: invalid variable name: ${z_varname}"
133-
134-  # Roll population (only persists in same-process context, lost in $() subshell)
135-  z_buz_varname_roll+=("${z_varname}")
136-  z_buz_colophon_roll+=("${z_colophon}")
137-  z_buz_module_roll+=("${z_module}")
138-  z_buz_command_roll+=("${z_command}")
139-  z_buz_channel_roll+=("${z_channel}")
140-  z_buz_describe_roll+=("${z_description}")
141-
142-  # Assign colophon to caller's variable
143-  printf -v "${z_varname}" '%s' "${z_colophon}" || buc_die "buz_enroll: printf -v failed for ${z_varname}"
144-}
145-
146-######################################################################
147-# Rust const projection (emit one pub const line per name/value pair)

input.description: See how theurge invokes tabtargets and passes env

content.content:
17:// RBTDRI — tabtarget invocation layer for theurge
19:// Theurge invokes bottle operations exclusively through tabtargets, never
25://   4. BURV fact file reading — extract structured output from tabtarget results
38:/// BUK dispatch output subdirectory — tabtargets write facts to BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR/current.

content.text:
Tree's clean and I've now read the real machinery — `buz_enroll`, the BURE tweak gate, and theurge's invocation layer. Here's the contrast, and my honest read is that your instinct is sound: the two approaches aren't actually rivals, they enforce against *different mistakes*, and the declarative one is the load-bearing layer.

## The docket's approach: poison at credential load

The fast runner exports a marker; `rba_rbra_load` (and presumably the OAuth mint) dies under it. What it really is: a **tripwire at the deepest chokepoint**. Its one genuine virtue is that it catches *lies* — any path that actually reaches credentials dies, no matter how it got there. Its weaknesses:

- **It's late.** The case has already executed arbitrary side-effectful code before it trips. It blocks the spend, not the run.
- **It's a sideband convention** — a private contract between theurge and the auth module, registered nowhere, discoverable only by reading both ends. That's exactly the shape of the non-load-bearing complexity you smelled this morning: a one-off env var with no home in any registry.
- **It only covers authenticated cloud.** Unauthenticated egress (the curl-to-Canonical fetch path) never touches RBRA load.

## Your approach: capability declared at enrollment, enforced at dispatch

`buz_enroll` currently carries six columns: varname, colophon, module, command, channel, description. The `channel` column is the precedent — enrollment already declares *how a colophon receives its folio*; a seventh column declaring *whether it may reach the cloud* is the same kind of fact in the same home. And there's even prior art in your own conventions: the zipper comments already say `UPPER=mutates GAR/cost, lower=read` — you've been hand-declaring a weaker version of this per-colophon all along, just not machine-readably.

The enforcement story is cleaner than you may have feared, because the architecture already gives you both halves:

- **One chokepoint exists.** Every tabtarget — including every sub-tabtarget a tabtarget calls — dispatches through the workbench's zipper lookup. The gate is a few lines there: if the constraint var says cloud-forbidden and the colophon's column says cloud, die *before anything runs*. Not at credential load — at the front door, pre-side-effects.
- **Propagation is free.** Theurge's `rbtdri_invoke_impl` already threads env vars into child processes (it sets `BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR`/`BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` on every call and has an `extra_env` slot). The fast tier sets the constraint once; the environment carries it down through any depth of sub-tabtarget calls, and each dispatch re-checks at its own front door. No per-call plumbing.
- **A static check falls out for free later.** The zipper already projects to `rbtdgc_consts.rs` and the tabtarget-context doc via the build. Project the cloud column too, and theurge can refuse a fast fixture that *references* a cloud colophon at suite-composition time — failure before any process even launches. That's a stage-2 option, not required for stage 1.

On the constraint variable itself: I'd note BURE's tweak slot (`BURE_TWEAK_NAME`) is probably the *wrong* home — that's the consumer-interpreted test-seam channel, single-slot, shape-validated only. A cloud-availability constraint is dispatch-layer policy, more akin to the `BURD_*`/`BURV_*` dispatch environment family: the dispatch machinery itself enforces it, consumers never interpret it. Naming is mint-time, but the family choice matters.

## The one real wrinkle

Your existing fast podvm-resolve case **deliberately invokes a cloud-capable colophon** (`rbw-lI`) in resolve-only mode via the `buorb_immure_resolve_only` seam. Under a pure dispatch gate, `rbw-lI` is cloud-marked and the fast case dies at the front door — correctly by policy, wrongly by intent. So the design needs an explicit answer: either the case carries a visible, greppable per-invocation override ("I know this colophon is cloud-marked; the seam keeps it local"), or that case migrates out of fast. This is also where the docket's poison earns a residual role: if you allow overrides, a 5-line credential-load tripwire is the backstop that catches an override whose seam silently stopped working. Belt (declarative gate) and suspenders (tripwire) — but the belt is the system; the tripwire shrinks from "the whole mechanism" to a footnote.

## What this means for the pace

- The cost profile inverts: instead of a small-but-sideband hack, you get a wide-but-mechanical diff — `buz_enroll` gains a column, ~100 enrollment lines get a one-word honest classification (which needs real reading, not haiku), plus the dispatch gate. The classification sweep is itself documentation value.
- Scope grows past RBK: `buz_enroll` is **BUK**, so this touches the kit boundary — which reinforces yesterday's conclusion that this pace doesn't belong in ₣BH's tail. It wants a reslate (this conversation is the docket material) and probably a restring.

Open questions I'd put to you before any reslate: binary `cloud`/`local` or worst-case classification for mode-dependent verbs; the override shape for resolve-only cases; whether stage-2 static composition checking is in-scope or an itch; and where the pace lives (₣BB still looks right to me, though the BUK touch could argue for its own small heat).

message.content: Well the split between BU and RB is really significant; BU doesn't have any cloud services...  I'm stumbling for a simple clean model here but perhaps tweaks _is_ the right place, and we have a tweak that says 'cloud disabled'.  Then, we have a BUZ member that is populated with some sort of sprue stating 'no constraint' in 'simple commands' in BU, same in RB, except for tabtargets that need cloud, which get an rb sprue saying 'needs cloud'.  Is this a path to a good plan?

content.text:
Yes — this is a path to a good plan, and the layering insight you just articulated is the piece that makes it clean. BUK ships the *mechanism* with no semantics; RB mints the *meaning*. That's exactly the established `buo` tweak philosophy: BURE validates shape only, never names, and `grep` is the registry. Your model extends that philosophy from tweak names to enrollment metadata. Let me sharpen it and flag two real decisions plus one collision.

## The model, stated precisely

- **`buz_enroll` gains a constraint column.** Value is either empty (no constraint) or a consumer-minted sprued token. BUK validates the sprue *shape* only — it never knows what "cloud" means. RB mints one token (say, under a new constraint-token sprue; mint-time naming) and stamps it on the ~30 colophons that genuinely reach GCP. Every BU enrollment, and most RB enrollments, carry empty.
- **A BURE-family environment variable carries the denial.** The fast suite runner exports "deny token X"; theurge already threads env into tabtarget children, and sub-tabtargets inherit it for free.
- **The dispatch chokepoint enforces by string-match.** At zipper lookup, if the colophon's enrolled token is in the denied set: die loud, naming the colophon and the token, before anything runs. Pure mechanism — BUK stays cloud-ignorant forever, and a future kit could mint its own tokens ("needs docker daemon", "needs display") without touching BUK.

## One concrete collision to design around

"Perhaps tweaks *is* the right place" is right about **ownership and philosophy**, but it can't be the *slot*: `BURE_TWEAK_NAME`/`VALUE` is a single name+value pair, and your near-miss case is the proof — the fast podvm-resolve case already occupies the tweak slot with `buorb_immure_resolve_only`, and it would need the cloud-denial active *simultaneously*. So the denial needs its own variable — a sibling living in BURE's regime under the same shape-only validation discipline, just not the existing tweak pair.

## Two decisions worth making deliberately

**Forced choice vs opt-in annotation.** If the constraint is a required 7th argument, every enrollment — including all of BUK's and the other kits' zippers — gets a mechanical `""`, and the diff is wide but every future enrollment author is *forced to answer the question*. If instead it's an opt-in follow-up call only cloud colophons make, the diff localizes to RB's ~30 lines — but absence-means-unconstrained is precisely the convention-not-construction failure the original memo recorded: a new cloud colophon whose author forgets the annotation silently passes the fast gate. I'd take the required argument. (Note even forced choice forces awareness, not honesty — a misclassified `""` still lies. That's the residual case where a 5-line credential-load tripwire stays useful as backstop.)

**What happens to the resolve-only fast case.** Under this model `rbw-lI` is enrolled needs-cloud, so the fast podvm-resolve case gets refused at dispatch — correctly by policy, against current intent. The clean resolution falls out of your own model: a tweak that flips a command from cloud to local is really *two commands wearing one colophon*, and the constraint column makes that visible. Give the resolve-only path its own unconstrained colophon (a real local command) and retire the seam-through-a-cloud-verb shape. Costs one colophon mint; buys back the gate's integrity with no override mechanism at all — which is the simplicity win, since any override channel reintroduces the honesty hole.

## What I'd carry into a reslate

The pace becomes: BUK mechanism (column + denial var + dispatch gate, shape-only), RB classification sweep (honest reading of ~100 enrollments, one word each), theurge fast tier exports the denial, the resolve-only colophon split, and the convention stated in the test-authoring context. The original docket's poison-marker design drops to an optional backstop line. And the BUK touch now firmly says this isn't ₣BH tail work — it wants restringing (₣BB, or splitting BUK-mechanism / RB-adoption across their natural homes).

Want me to draft the reslate docket along these lines?

message.content: Well it is a surprise to me that tweaks grew that immure member.  Is it well used?  I'm pretty inclined to evict it, use tweaks for now for this, until we know the shape of other similar but non-overlapping constraints.

content.text:
Short answer: the immure seam is barely used — it's days old (landed with the podvm refresh pace on 2026-06-10, the same night as the memo), has exactly one consumer (the fast podvm-resolve case), and eviction is cheap. But the grep turned up something that matters for your plan: **the tweak slot has two other live occupants** you should know about before committing it to constraint duty.

## Current tweak-slot census

Three RB tweaks exist, each set per-invocation by a theurge fixture:

1. **`buorb_immure_resolve_only`** — `rbldv_Immure.sh:212` short-circuits after resolve; the fast podvm-resolve case sets it and asserts the marker line in output. The eviction candidate.
2. **An ensconce stamp-pin tweak** — `rbldb_Bole.sh:217`; the *crucible*-tier lode tests use it to pin the Lode touchmark.
3. **A graft image-override tweak** — `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1535`; the onboarding graft track (gauntlet tier) uses it.

The good news for your plan: those other two live in crucible/gauntlet tiers, which are cloud-using by design. Only the immure seam occupies the slot *inside the fast tier*. So evicting it alone genuinely frees the slot for the duration of a fast run — you don't need to disturb the other two.

## The eviction

The cleanest eviction is the colophon split we discussed: give the resolve-only path its own read-only, unconstrained colophon. What's now a test seam mutilating a cloud verb becomes an honest local command — effectively a dry-run/plan verb ("show me what immure would capture"), which has operator value independent of testing. The fast case invokes that colophon directly, no tweak, and its marker-assertion trick is replaced by simply being a different command. `rbldv_Immure.sh` keeps the resolve logic; only the short-circuit-via-tweak shape retires.

## Tweak-as-constraint, kept shape-clean

There's a way to ride the tweak slot without breaking BURE's "BUK never knows consumer names" discipline: the dispatch gate doesn't interpret any specific name — it just refuses dispatch **when `BURE_TWEAK_NAME` equals the colophon's enrolled constraint token**. A seam tweak like the graft override matches no enrollment column, so it flows through untouched; the cloud token matches the ~30 cloud enrollments, so the fast runner setting `BURE_TWEAK_NAME=buorb_cloud_…` (buo-sprued, satisfying the existing shape gate) denies exactly those. BUK stays a string-comparator; RB minted both sides of the match.

## The trade you're accepting

Single-slot means **no fast case can use a seam tweak while the constraint occupies the slot**. Today, post-eviction, that set is empty — the trade costs nothing. The day a fast case legitimately needs a seam *and* the constraint, that's the forcing event to mint the dedicated constraint variable, and the migration is mechanical (the enrollment column and tokens survive; only the env var carrying the denial changes). I'd state that explicitly in the reslate as the demolition condition — it's a membrane with a named retirement trigger, which keeps it honest rather than calcifying.

So the revised pace shape: evict the immure seam via the resolve-only colophon split, add the constraint column to `buz_enroll` (required arg, `""` for unconstrained), the generic tweak-match gate at dispatch, RB's classification sweep, fast runner exports the token, convention documented. Want the reslate drafted?

message.content: Groom heat Be because I think it has a bearing on this: I expressly intended (though probably didn't assure solid documentation about) tweaks _only_ to force hard to produce conditions to prove that the infrastructure does the right thing.  Ignoring that immure tweak (dreamed up by LLM), is that consistent with the other ones?  I'm thinking anything important enough to observe a tweak will also need a named negative.  These also won't be 'deep' tabtargets, generally.

Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 64%
Heat: rbk-09-better-negatives (₣Be)
Status: stabled
Created: 260610


## Paces

### band-tinder-and-die-membrane (₢BeAAA) [rough]

Establish the precision exit-code band and make `buc_die` transparent to it.

Define band tinder in `Tools/buk/bubc_constants.sh`:
band base/width plus per-gate rejection codes —
per rejection gate, never per validation rule.
Pick the band clear of shell-reserved codes (2, 126, 127, 128+n).

Teach `buc_die` (`Tools/buk/buc_command.sh`) to capture `$?` on entry
and re-exit with it when the value lies in the band;
add an origin helper for deliberate rejection sites.
Existing `cmd || buc_die` call sites stay untouched —
the membrane makes them propagate band codes for free.

Prove end-to-end survival:
a BUK self-test case where a band code raised under a `cmd || buc_die` chain
reaches the caller's captured exit status through the tabtarget/launcher exec path.

## Cinched
Exit-code band over stderr sentinel;
per-gate codes, not per-rule.

## Done when
Band tinder and membrane land;
`tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green including the new survival case.

### numeric-const-codegen (₢BeAAB) [rough]

Project the band constants into Rust as numeric consts.

Extend the rbcc emission (`Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh`, riding
`rbz_emit_consts` in `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` → `rbtdgc_consts.rs`)
with a numeric emit shape — current emission is `&str` only,
theurge compares exit codes as integers.
bubc is sourced at emission time,
so projecting BUK-homed band values needs no cross-module tinder trick.
Write-on-change and the qualify freshness gate apply unchanged.

## Done when
Generated `rbtdgc_consts.rs` carries the band as numeric consts;
`tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` regenerates it and `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh` passes freshness.

### migrate-regime-validation-negatives (₢BeAAC) [rough]

Close the wrong-reason hole for the regime-validation fixture's negative cases.

Teach the regime probate gate to reject with its band code via the origin helper.
Note regime probate co-occurs with nearly every CLI verb's pipeline,
so per the allocation rule it keeps a code of its own.
Extend the theurge negative helpers (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`)
to assert a specific expected code rather than bare nonzero,
and flip the regime-validation fixture's negative cases onto it.

## Done when
`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` green;
a deliberate harness-style breakage (e.g. unbound variable in the gate path)
now fails the negative case instead of passing it.

### migrate-enrollment-validation-negatives (₢BeAAD) [rough]

Close the wrong-reason hole for the enrollment-validation fixture's negative cases.

Same shape as the regime-validation migration:
teach the enrollment validation gate to reject with its band code,
flip the fixture's negative cases to assert that specific code.
The expect-code plumbing in the theurge negative helpers
lands in the prior migration pace; this pace consumes it.

## Done when
`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` green
with every enrollment-validation negative case asserting the gate's code.

### migrate-recipe-and-hygiene-negatives (₢BeAAE) [rough]

Close the wrong-reason hole for the recipe-validation
and dockerfile-hygiene fixtures' negative cases.

Teach the recipe validation gate and the rbfh FROM-line hygiene gate
(`Tools/rbk/rbfh_FoundryHygiene.sh`) to reject with band codes.
These two gates are alternatives — never in one spawn path —
so they may share a code per the allocation rule;
confirm that at migration time rather than assuming.
Flip both fixtures' negative cases to assert the expected code.

## Done when
`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` green
with both fixtures' negative cases asserting specific codes.

### bcg-precision-band-admission (₢BeAAF) [rough]

Write the BCG admission entry for the precision exit-code band.

State the design position:
differentiated exit codes are bad design, interpreting error strings is worse,
so a small per-gate band exists for exactly the tabtargets
the test orchestrator asserts negatively — nothing else.
Include: band semantics (in-band means deliberate rejection,
code 1 stays "imprecise death"),
the allocation rule (share across alternatives, never along a pipeline),
the enrollment requirement (no code minted outside the bubc tinder block),
and the rejected stderr-sentinel alternative with why it lost.
Cross-reference from the bubc tinder block comment.

## Character
Prose craft against a settled design — judgment in wording, not in shape.

## Done when
BCG carries the entry; bubc tinder block points at it.

File-touch bitmap: (no work file changes)

# jjezs_paddock ₣Be

## Gate

Do not start this heat until ₣Bb's bash-filename case-consolidation pace
(now first in that heat) has landed.
That pace renames PascalCase bash files across `Tools/rbk` —
including files this heat edits directly
(the rbcc constants module, the foundry-hygiene gate, the validation gate homes) —
and rename-on-one-side, edit-on-the-other is the worst git merge shape.
Once it lands, this heat runs comfortably in a parallel repo alongside the rest of ₣Bb;
verify the rename pace is wrapped via `jjx_show` on ₣Bb before mounting here.
Mind that pace's renames when reading this heat's dockets:
file references here predate the consolidation,
so resolve any stale PascalCase basename to its lowercase successor.

## Shape

Theurge negative cases assert bare nonzero exit,
so a case expecting deliberate rejection also passes on any harness breakage
(unbound variable, missing file, refactor typo) —
~60 negative cases across the fast-tier fixtures carry this wrong-reason hole.
The fix: a small precision exit-code band, allocated per rejection gate
(roughly half a dozen codes), homed as tinder in bubc,
projected into the theurge Rust consts by the existing zipper codegen,
and asserted specifically by the negative helpers.

Work order: membrane first (band tinder + `buc_die` band-propagation + survival proof),
then numeric codegen, then per-fixture migrations, then the BCG admission entry.

## Cinched

- Exit-code band over stderr sentinel:
  a sentinel is string interpretation minimized, not avoided,
  and is swallowed by the same wrappers that launder codes.
- Per-gate codes, never per-rule —
  the hole being closed is wrong-layer failure, not wrong-rule failure.
- Allocation rule: gates may share a code only if they never co-occur
  in one test case's spawn path — share across alternatives, never along a pipeline.
- `buc_die` propagates in-band `$?` values instead of remapping to 1,
  so existing `|| buc_die` call sites need no audit or change.
- No code minted outside the bubc tinder block.

## Out of scope

tadmor-security's containment cases:
exit codes there cross docker-exec boundaries with their own laundering rules —
a different problem, a different heat if it ever itches.

## Done when

Every fast-tier negative case asserts a specific band code,
the survival proof lives in the BUK self-test,
and the BCG admission entry documents band semantics and the allocation rule.

reaches the caller's captured exit status through the tabtarget/launcher exec path.

## Cinched
Exit-code band over stderr sentinel;
per-gate codes, not per-rule.

## Done when

`tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green including the new survival case.

# jjezs_pace ₢BeAAB

Project the band constants into Rust as numeric consts.

Extend the rbcc emission (`Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh`, riding
`rbz_emit_consts` in `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` → `rbtdgc_consts.rs`)
with a numeric emit shape — current emission is `&str` only,

# jjezs_pace ₢BeAAC

content.text:
₣Be in one sentence: a stabled six-pace heat that closes the "wrong-reason hole" — theurge negative cases assert bare nonzero, so they pass on *any* harness breakage — by minting a small per-gate exit-code band in BUK (`bubc` tinder), making `buc_die` transparent to it, projecting it into theurge as numeric consts, and migrating the fast-tier fixtures to assert specific codes. It's gated on ₣Bb's filename-consolidation pace landing first.

## Your doctrine vs. the tweak census

Checking the two non-immure tweaks against "tweaks exist *only* to force hard-to-produce conditions to prove the infrastructure does the right thing":

**`buorb_ensconce_stamp` — the archetype, fully consistent.** The bash comment states the doctrine almost verbatim: the time-based stamp mint is seconds-grained, so two CLI ensconces *never* naturally share a touchmark — the cloud-side collision guard's idempotent and collision branches **can never fire without the pin**. The crucible case then drives both branches deliberately: same digest under a pinned touchmark (positive control, must pass) and a different-base ensconce onto the same touchmark (must be rejected). That's exactly "force the unproducible condition, observe the named refusal."

**`buorb_graft_image` — not consistent.** It injects `RBRV_GRAFT_IMAGE` so the onboarding graft case can point the vessel at a test-controlled image without editing committed regime config. That's *parameter injection to enable a positive path*, not condition-forcing to prove a guard — and it rides through `ordain`, a deep tabtarget, contradicting your "won't be deep" expectation on both counts. The doctrinally clean shapes would be a CLI parameter on the graft path or a test-owned vessel regime; worth a disposition of its own (itch-grade, not urgent).

So: one of three complies. Your stated intent was never documented anywhere I can find — BUS0/the BUK include describe the tweak *mechanism* (shape validation, grep-as-registry) but are silent on what tweaks are *for*. That silence is precisely how the immure and graft uses drifted in.

## And yes — your "named negative" instinct is what ties ₣Be to the cloud gate

The ensconce-stamp tweak proves your claim empirically: the only reason to force a condition is to observe the refusal, and a refusal worth observing deserves a precise identity. ₣Be is the vocabulary for that — band codes per rejection gate. The convergence with the cloud constraint is clean:

- The dispatch cloud-gate **is a rejection gate in ₣Be's exact sense**, and it homes beautifully: the gate lives in BUK dispatch, and the band tinder lives in BUK's `bubc` — so the *refusal code* is BUK's, while the *token semantics* stay RB's. No cross-kit smear.
- The gate then gets its own doctrine-compliant proof case: a fast fixture deliberately invokes a cloud colophon under the denial and asserts the gate's specific band code — named negative, not bare nonzero. That case is the only *condition-forcing* use; the suite-wide export is operational protection by the same gate.
- Your "not deep" observation also resolves: seam tweaks are read by command bodies, so depth matters; the constraint is read by the dispatch layer at *every* level, so it's depth-independent by construction — the right property for policy, and another hint it's a different species from seam tweaks.

That last point is the honest tension to hold: under your strict doctrine, the standing fast-suite denial isn't really a *tweak* — it's policy riding the tweak carrier for now, with the demolition condition we named (first fast case needing seam + constraint simultaneously forces the dedicated variable).

## Sequencing consequence

The constraint gate wants ₢BeAAA/₢BeAAB (band tinder + numeric codegen) underneath it so it can mint its code from day one rather than landing as bare `buc_die` and migrating later. ₣Be is stabled and gated on ₣Bb's rename pace. So the emerging order is: ₣Bb rename pace → ₣Be membrane paces → the cloud-constraint pace (wherever it lives) → its classification sweep. If that order feels right, the structural moves would be: reslate ₢BHAAl into the constraint shape and restring it (₣BB or ₣Be's neighborhood), slate the graft-tweak disposition as an itch, and consider a small pace somewhere to *write the tweak doctrine down* — BUS0 or the BUK include — since its absence is how this drift happened.

content.text:
Got it — and the history is kinder to you than the doctrine is. The rationale was real, and it's a *third* species: dynamic-value chaining.

## The original rationale (2026-03-15, ₢AtAAP in ₣At rbk-mvp-3-add-graft)

The commit says it directly: the ThreeMode test ran an end-to-end **conjure → retrieve → graft** chain. It read the GAR_ROOT/ARK_STEM facts from the conjure step's output, pulled the just-conjured image down to local Docker, and then needed to tell graft "graft *this* image" — **a value minted seconds earlier, mid-test**. A runtime-minted value can't live in committed vessel config, and tools-never-commit forbids the test writing `rbrv.env`. The tweak was the only clean channel for threading a dynamic value into a slot that real operators author statically. That's a defensible engineering call — but it's injection-for-chaining, not condition-forcing, so your judgment today is right that it sits outside the principle.

## The rationale has since lapsed

Two pieces of evidence:

1. **The modern consumer injects a constant.** Today's onboarding graft case docker-pulls `busybox:latest` (a fixed const, `RBTDRO_GRAFT_SOURCE_IMAGE`) and tweaks *that* in. There's no dynamic chain anymore — the conjure→graft handoff this tweak existed to carry is gone from the surviving test.
2. **The committed regime slot holds a fossil.** `rbev-graft-demo/rbrv.env` carries `RBRV_GRAFT_IMAGE=test_busybox:local-20251231-081227-44143-75` — a dynamically-minted tag from some December run, dead weight that nothing reads because the tweak always overrides it under test and no operator flow exercises it.

So this is the Palisade lesson in miniature: a membrane built against a real grievance (dynamic chaining), grievance healed (the chain test retired), membrane calcified because it carried no demolition condition.

## The cheap cleanup

Set `RBRV_GRAFT_IMAGE=busybox:latest` (or a digest-pinned form) in the committed graft-demo regime, keep the test's docker-pull priming, delete the tweak read in `rbfd` and the injection in `rbtdro` — behavior identical, one tweak gone. After that plus the immure eviction, your tweak census is exactly one: the ensconce-stamp archetype, which *is* your doctrine. The doctrine statement you'd write into BUS0 then describes reality, not aspiration: a tweak forces a hard-to-produce condition so a named negative can prove the refusal; anything else wants a parameter, a regime slot, or a colophon of its own.

That's itch-or-small-pace grade — it could ride wherever the constraint work lands, or stand alone. Want me to capture it (and the doctrine-documentation line) somewhere now, or hold it for the reslate conversation?

content.text:
Yes — and it's a better repair than the one I proposed. Mine was the minimal patch (commit the now-static value into the regime); yours is the structural one, and the codebase is further along toward it than I expected. Two findings from the dig:

**The producer half already exists.** Ordain *already* emits exactly the fact you're describing: `rbfd` writes `rbf_fact_hallmark`, `rbf_fact_gar_root`, and `rbf_fact_ark_stem` on every conjure/bind/graft, and kludge mirrors the same trio (`rbfk_kludge.sh:226` — "mirror conjure/bind/graft so downstream consumers"). The hallmark fact is sitting in `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR` after every ordain today.

**The chain-reader pattern is already live — in ordain itself.** Bole election is precisely this shape running in production: ensconce leaves the touchmark + kind-brand facts, and the *next* tabtarget (ordain) reads them from `BURD_PREVIOUS_DIR` via the ₢BHAAQ chaining machinery (`rbfd:910–937`). Capture leaves a fact; the consumer chains off it; the election survives independent retry.

So what's missing is only the piece you named: the **installer** — an election verb that chains off the hallmark fact and writes the consumer slot. Today that election happens in-process inside the cycle tabtargets (rbw-tK/rbw-tO drive the hallmark into the nameplate by function composition), which is the bundling the ₣BH paddock explicitly tolerates in `tt/` while insisting the verbs stay pure. A hallmark installer would be the made-side sibling of `yoke`: lowercase, consumer-family, operator-committed, reads the chained fact, writes the slot.

Mapped onto the graft tweak specifically: the original ThreeMode grievance was "conjure's output must become graft's input, and graft's input channel is committed config." With the installer, that chain becomes ordain (emits hallmark fact) → summon/retrieve (localizes) → **installer writes `RBRV_GRAFT_IMAGE`** → graft reads its regime as authored. No tweak, no test-only channel — the test exercises the same verbs an operator would use, which is strictly more honest than the tweak ever was. And the current simplified test needs even less: its value is static, so the regime carries it directly and the fossil value gets replaced.

The convergence is satisfying: this is the ₣BH paddock's own capture/election split arriving at its declared destination — the paddock already defers "retrofitting made-images" to a sibling made-side heat (where Ark earns its `k` family). The hallmark installer is a natural first pace of that heat, or a small standalone pace if you want the graft tweak retired sooner. Either way, after immure eviction + graft-tweak retirement, the tweak census lands at exactly one — the ensconce-stamp archetype — and the doctrine you stated becomes a description of reality.

Worth capturing while it's warm: the installer pace (with the graft-tweak retirement riding it), the doctrine line for BUS0, and the constraint-pace reslate from earlier in this conversation. Want me to draft those?

content.text:
This hangs together well — and the dig just confirmed the "weird theurge machinery" is even weirder than you remembered, which strengthens the case. Thoughts in order of weight:

## What "bleeding out" actually removes

The current regime-validation negatives work by **fabricating regime files in Rust** (`rbtdrf_run_probate_in` writes synthetic env bodies) and driving them through **twelve dedicated `*_probate` side doors** — test-only CLI entries grown onto every regime module (`rbrr_probate`, `rbrn_probate`, `burc`/`burn`/`burp`/`burs` variants, the lot). Your in-universe shape replaces both: run the *real* validate colophons (`rbw-rnv`, `rbw-rvv`, `rbw-rrv`… — they already exist as the operator surface) against the *real committed regime*, with a poison tweak bleeding one bad value in after load, asserting the gate's specific band code. The probate doors and the Rust file-fabrication both delete. That's a genuine load-bearing-complexity win, not just tidiness.

And the poison tweak is **doctrine-pure by your own statement**: an invalid value inside an otherwise-valid real regime is a hard-to-produce condition precisely because committed regimes are valid and tools never commit broken config. It becomes the second archetype next to `buorb_ensconce_stamp`, and both observe named negatives. The doctrine describes the whole census.

## Three things to get right

**Don't double-touch the fixtures.** The existing ₢BeAAC/₢BeAAD/₢BeAAE are slated as "flip existing probate-harness cases to assert codes." Under the new gestalt those cases don't migrate — they're *replaced*. Migrating them to band codes first and converting to poison second touches every case twice. I'd reslate those three paces as the conversion itself: in-universe poison + specific code + delete that fixture's probate doors.

**Census the cases by grain before promising full bleed-out.** Post-load value poisoning can't produce *file-grain* failures — missing file, unparseable line, unknown key. Some of the 21 regime + 47 enrollment negatives are surely file-grain and will keep a small fabrication path (or get an explicit "covered elsewhere" verdict). The conversion pace should open with that census rather than assuming 100% conversion; otherwise the probate deletion stalls halfway.

**The graft conversion has a scope fork.** "Chaining without tweak" in its full form mints the hallmark-installer verb — made-side election machinery, which the ₣BH paddock charts for the made-side retrofit heat, not a negatives heat. The minimal repair (static `busybox:latest` in the committed graft-demo regime, delete both tweak ends) retires the tweak now with zero new verbs; the installer can then land in its proper heat and the chain-shaped test returns with it. I'd do minimal-in-Be, installer-elsewhere — but that's an operator call.

## Sequencing and small adjacencies

Order looks right with one note: immure eviction and graft retirement are band-independent, so they can lead while ₢BeAAA/AAB stay first-among-the-band-dependent — the poison conversions are the only paces that genuinely *need* the band underneath. Also worth deciding while reslating:

- The **poison seam location** wants to be one membrane in BUK's regime-load path (post-source, pre-validate), so all regimes — BUK's and RB's — get it from one seam. Mint-time detail, but it's the thing that keeps this from becoming twelve little seams.
- The **paddock needs the gestalt shift written in**: Be's shape grows from "precision exit codes" to "doctrine-pure negative testing — tweak census cleanup, named refusal codes, in-universe poisoning, probate-door demolition." The tweak doctrine statement (BUS0) naturally rides this heat too — maybe folded into ₢BeAAF's prose pace.
- The **cloud-constraint pace stays unhomed** by this plan — noting it, not pushing it. Its band-code dependency is now being built in Be, so terminal-in-Be is defensible, as is ₣BB. Can stay open until the reslate.

One ordering caution from the existing paddock: Be is gated on ₣Bb's filename-consolidation pace, and these new paces deepen the overlap (they touch the same validation-gate homes that pace renames). The gate holds for the additions too.

When you're ready to draft, I have everything from this conversation to write the reslates and new dockets in one pass.

message.content:
I realize we didn't determine the resolution at heat Be resolution concerning the cloud tweak.  What if the tweak is simply one that makes any attempt at retrieving an oauth token instantly fatal?  Then we don't even need to change the zippers. 

If you judge that effective as a replacement for the point of this current pace, then please do take that census of current theurge regime cases now.  I do trust they boil down to a small set of known error codes and potentially tweak shapes.

After that, assess: do we have a strong plan that abandons current pace but recreates all the steps we just discussed in heat Bb?

content.text:
Full circle, and I think rightly so — the OAuth-fatal tweak is essentially the original docket's poison marker, but it's no longer a sideband hack now that the rest of the structure exists around it. Quick judgment, then I'll take the census.

**Judgment: yes, it's an effective replacement.** Every spend-or-mutate path in this codebase must mint a token first — JWT SA or Payor OAuth, all through the `rbgo`/`rba` chokepoint — so a tweak making token mint instantly fatal (with its own band code, once ₣Be lands) gates the *actual* hazard rather than a declared classification. What changes versus the zipper design: you lose fail-at-dispatch-before-side-effects (acceptable — pre-token side effects are BURV-isolated local work) and gain zero zipper churn, no 100-line classification sweep, and **no misclassification hole** — the gate can't be lied to, because it sits on real credential use, not declared capability. The one residual: unauthenticated host egress isn't covered, but that's outside the hazard class (no spend, no depot mutation) and the trust model forbids workstation acquisition anyway. Depth is also a non-issue: env propagates to every sub-process, and token mint is a chokepoint at any depth. Doctrine-wise it rides as the standing-protection use of a gate whose *proof case* is doctrine-pure (set tweak, invoke a cloud verb, assert the code).

Now the census — this is a 75-case read across three fixtures, so I'm sending a thorough explorer at it.

input.prompt:
Census the negative test cases in /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs (theurge fast-tier fixtures). Search breadth: very thorough on this one file, plus targeted reads of the bash probate entries it calls.

Context: these fixtures port enrollment-validation (47 cases), regime-validation (21 cases), and regime-smoke (7 cases) from an old bash test framework. The negative cases currently work by fabricating synthetic regime env-file bodies in Rust (see rbtdrf_run_probate_in around line 197) and driving them through dedicated bash `*_probate` side-door entry points (rbrr_probate, rbrn_probate, rbrv_probate, rbrd_probate, rbro_probate, rbrp_probate, rbrs_probate, rbra_probate, burc_probate, burn_probate, burp_probate, burs_probate — names per rbtdrm_manifest.rs constants RBTDRM_PROBATE_*). They assert bare nonzero exit.

A redesign is being planned: replace fabricated-file probate harnesses with "in-universe" testing — run the real validate CLI verbs against the real committed regimes, with a BURE tweak that poisons one named regime variable to an invalid value after load but before validation, asserting a specific exit code per rejection gate.

Deliverable — a census answering:

1. For each fixture (enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, and also recipe-validation and dockerfile-hygiene if they use similar fabrication), how many cases are positive vs negative?

2. Classify every NEGATIVE case into grain categories:
   - VALUE-grain: stages a syntactically-fine regime file where one variable carries an invalid VALUE (bad enum, out-of-range number, malformed string, empty required field, etc.) → convertible to the poison-tweak shape.
   - FILE-grain: missing file, unreadable file, unparseable/garbage content, unknown/extra keys → NOT convertible to value poisoning; needs file fabrication or another shape.
   - OTHER: argument errors, structural/cross-variable consistency failures, anything else — describe briefly.
   Don't list all ~75 cases individually unless ambiguous; give per-fixture counts per category, with a few representative case names each, and individually list every FILE-grain and OTHER case.

3. Identify the distinct REJECTION GATES the negative cases exercise. Look at what bash function actually dies: e.g., buv_validation.sh rules (buv_string/buv_number range checks) vs regime probate orchestration vs enrollment validation vs something else. Read the relevant bash: Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh, Tools/buk/burc_regime.sh (and one or two of the rb regime modules like Tools/rbk/rbrn_*/rbrv_* if needed) to name the gate layer for each category. The redesign allocates exit codes PER GATE (not per rule), so the answer wanted is: how many distinct gates do these 75 cases boil down to, and which?

4. Note what the enrollment-validation fixture actually validates (is it buv enrollment rules — string/number/path enrollment — rather than regime files?) and whether the poison-tweak shape even applies to it, or whether it's a different beast.

5. Note any negative case that asserts on stderr text rather than exit code.

Report as a compact structured summary. Include file:line references for the key harness functions and a representative case of each category.

content.text:
Good! Now let me compile all the information I've gathered. Let me create a comprehensive summary:

Based on my thorough analysis of the test file and the bash probate entry points, here is the census:

## NEGATIVE TEST CASE CENSUS

### 1. FIXTURE CASE COUNTS (Positive vs Negative)

**Enrollment-Validation (47 cases total)**
- Positive: 18 cases
- Negative: 29 cases (all VALUE-grain)
  - Distributed across: string (3), xname (4), gname (3), fqin (2), bool (4), enum (3), decimal (3), ipv4 (2), port (3), odref (4), list_string (2), list_ipv4 (1), list_gname (1), gate (1), enforce (3), report (1)

**Regime-Validation (48 cases total)**
- Positive: 12 baseline-valid + 3 discovery cases (15 total)
- Negative: 33 cases
  - VALUE-grain: 19 cases
  - FILE-grain: 13 cases
  - OTHER: 1 case

**Regime-Smoke (10 cases total)**
- Positive: 9 cases
- Negative: 1 case (empty-arg refusal - OTHER type, cross-cutting)

**Dockerfile-Hygiene (9 cases total)**
- Positive: 5 cases
- Negative: 3 cases (VALUE-grain)
- Other: 1 (all-vessels iteration)

**Recipe-Validation (10 cases total)**
- Positive: 6 cases
- Negative: 4 cases (all VALUE-grain)

---

### 2. NEGATIVE CASE CLASSIFICATION

#### **ENROLLMENT-VALIDATION: 29 negative cases — ALL VALUE-GRAIN**
All use `RbtdrfSub::fatal()` which stages invalid values in enrolled variables and asserts bare `buv_vet` exit non-zero. Examples:
- `rbtdrf_ev_string_too_short`: value `"ab"` against min 5 → BUV length check
- `rbtdrf_ev_bool_invalid` / `"string true"`: value `"true"` instead of 0/1 → BUV bool enum
- `rbtdrf_ev_enum_invalid` / `"not a choice"`: value `"production"` not in `debug release test` → BUV enum check
- `rbtdrf_ev_decimal_below`: value `0` with min 1 → BUV decimal range
- `rbtdrf_ev_odref_malformed`: wrong sha256 algorithm or short hex → BUV odref regex
- `rbtdrf_ev_enforce_first_bad` (3 subs): middle var invalid, last var invalid → multiple BUV checks

**Rejection Gate**: `buv_vet` (enrolled type-specific checks in `zbuv_check_capture`, lines 365-600 of buv_validation.sh)

---

#### **REGIME-VALIDATION: 33 negative cases**

**VALUE-GRAIN (19 cases):**
All poison a variable in a synthetically-written regime .env file, then probate it.

1. **RBRR (8 VALUE-grain):**
   - `rbrr-bad-timeout`: `RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT="1200"` (missing 's' suffix) → regex check in `zrbrr_enforce` (rbrr_regime.sh, custom format)
   - `rbrr-bad-runtime-prefix-uppercase`: `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX="BAD-"` (uppercase) → regex `^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$` in `zrbrr_enforce`
   - `rbrr-bad-runtime-prefix-no-trailing-hyphen`: `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX="acme"` → same regex gate
   - `rbrr-bad-runtime-prefix-too-long`: `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX="twelvechars-"` (13 chars, max 11) → BUV string length in kindle + regex in enforce
   - `rbrr-bad-vessel-dir`: nonexistent path → `buv_dir_exists` in `zrbrr_enforce` (FILE-grain)
   - `rbrr-bad-secrets-dir`: nonexistent path → `buv_dir_exists` in `zrbrr_enforce` (FILE-grain)
   - `rbrr-unexpected-var`: extra `RBRR_BOGUS` var → `buv_scope_sentinel` in `zrbrr_kindle` (OTHER)

   → Rejection gates: BUV string/ipv4 checks (zbuv_check_capture), custom regex format checks, directory existence, scope sentinel

2. **RBRD (6 VALUE-grain):**
   - `rbrd-missing-moniker`: `unset RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` (required) → buv_vet sentinel (VALUE)
   - `rbrd-bad-moniker`: `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER="BAD-MONIKER"` (uppercase, hyphens) → regex `^[a-z][a-z0-9]*$` in `zrbrd_enforce` (VALUE)
   - `rbrd-bad-cloud-prefix-uppercase`: `RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX="BAD-"` → regex `^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$` (VALUE)
   - `rbrd-bad-cloud-prefix-no-trailing-hyphen`: `RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX="acme"` → same regex (VALUE)
   - `rbrd-bad-cloud-prefix-too-long`: `RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX="twelvechars-"` (13 chars) → BUV string length + regex (VALUE)

   → Rejection gates: BUV enrollment checks, custom regex format checks, joint-length cross-variable check

3. **RBRV (2 VALUE-grain, 3 FILE-grain):**
   - VALUE: `rbrv-missing-sigil`: required field unset → BUV vet
   - FILE: `rbrv-no-bind-image`: required in bind mode → BUV vet (missing required)
   - FILE: `rbrv-partial-conjure`: unset `RBRV_CONJURE_PLATFORMS` → BUV vet (mode-dependent)
   - FILE: `rbrv-unexpected-var` → scope sentinel (OTHER)

4. **RBRN (3 VALUE-grain, 1 OTHER):**
   - VALUE: `rbrn-missing-moniker`, `rbrn-invalid-runtime`, `rbrn-invalid-entry-mode`, `rbrn-invalid-dns-mode`, `rbrn-invalid-access-mode` → BUV enum checks
   - OTHER: `rbrn-port-conflict`: cross-variable consistency (entry ports < uplink min when enabled) → custom enforce check, not value poisoning
   - FILE: `rbrn-bad-ip`: `RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP="not-an-ip"` → BUV ipv4 format check (VALUE)

5. **RBRP (1 VALUE-grain):**
   - `rbrp-bad-payor-project`: regex against RBGC_GLOBAL_PAYOR_REGEX (requires prelude) → custom enforce check

6. **RBRS (1 VALUE-grain):**
   - `rbrs-missing-platform`: unset enum field → BUV vet

7. **RBRO (1 VALUE-grain, actually FILE):**
   - `rbro-missing-refresh-token`: unset field → BUV vet (VALUE per buv_vet, but functionally required field)

8. **RBRA (1 VALUE-grain):**
   - `rbra-bad-private-key`: `"no-pem-material"` without BEGIN marker → custom check in enforce

---

**FILE-GRAIN (13 cases):**

1. `rbrr-bad-vessel-dir`: /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbrr_regime.sh:~71, `buv_dir_exists "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}"`
2. `rbrr-bad-secrets-dir`: same file, `buv_dir_exists "${RBRR_SECRETS_DIR}"`
3. `rbrv-no-bind-image`: mode-dependent required field (FILE)
4. `rbrv-partial-conjure`: mode-dependent required subset (FILE)
5. `burc-missing-station-file`: /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/burc_regime.sh, `buv_file_exists` (FILE)
6. `burn-bad-platform`: actually VALUE (enum "bunne_solaris" invalid) — RECLASSIFY as VALUE
7. `burp-missing-workload-key`: /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/burp_regime.sh, unset required (VALUE per buv)
8. (removed: burn-bad-platform is VALUE)

**OTHER (1 case):**
- `rbrn-port-conflict`: cross-variable consistency enforcement (not poisonable via single var tweak)
- `rbrr-unexpected-var`: scope sentinel catch (not poisonable — it's an extra var not a bad value)
- `rbrn-unexpected-var`: same

---

#### **REGIME-SMOKE: 1 negative case**

- `rbtdrf_rs_unmake_empty_arg_refusal` (lines 1665-1710): Tabtarget refusal test — not regime-based; asserts `rbw-dU` dies non-zero when called with no argument. **Type: OTHER** (argument contract, not value poisoning). Does **NOT** assert stderr text; asserts exit code + combined stdout/stderr contains pointer string.

---

#### **DOCKERFILE-HYGIENE: 3 negative cases — ALL VALUE-GRAIN**

All synthetic Dockerfile bodies:
- `dh-reject-hardcoded-literal`: `FROM python:3.12-slim` (literal image, not parameterized) → rbw-fhc check
- `dh-reject-tab-in-from`: `FROM\t${RBF_IMAGE_1}` (tab in FROM line) → rbw-fhc check
- `dh-reject-trailing-backslash`: `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1} \` (continuation) → rbw-fhc check

**Rejection Gate**: rbw-fhc (Dockerfile-hygiene-check tabtarget) — inline parsing of FROM line format

---

#### **RECIPE-VALIDATION: 4 negative cases — ALL VALUE-GRAIN**

All inject invalid substitution keys or bodies:
- `rc-reject-missing-key`: keys file `_RBGL_A`, body `${_RBGL_B}` (uncovered) → zrbld_spine_validate
- `rc-reject-substring`: keys `_RBGL_TAG_BOLE`, body `${_RBGL_TAG}` (substring match fails) → zrbld_spine_validate
- `rc-reject-multi-second`: keys `_RBGL_A`, body `${_RBGL_A}/${_RBGL_B}` (second uncovered) → zrbld_spine_validate
- `rc-reject-empty-keys-ref`: keys empty, body `${_RBGL_A}` (uncovered) → zrbld_spine_validate

**Rejection Gate**: zrbld_spine_validate (substitution-key coverage check, rblds_Spine.sh)

---

### 3. DISTINCT REJECTION GATES

**Count: 8 distinct gates**

1. **BUV type-specific checks** (buv_validation.sh, `zbuv_check_capture`, lines 365-600)
   - Validates string/xname/gname/fqin/bool/enum/decimal/ipv4/port/odref/list_* according to enrolled type & params
   - Used by: enrollment-validation (all 29), regime-validation (RBRR, RBRD, RBRV base, RBRN base, RBRS, RBRO, RBRA)
   - Covers: length bounds, pattern regex, value enum, numeric range, format regex

2. **BUV enrollment sentinel** (buv_validation.sh, `buv_scope_sentinel`, lines 613-645)
   - Dies if any PREFIX_* vars exist not enrolled in scope
   - Used by: regime-validation (RBRR, RBRD, RBRV, RBRN, RBRO, RBRA, BURC, BURN, BURP, BURS)
   - Example: `rbrr-unexpected-var`, `rbrn-unexpected-var`

3. **Custom format checks in zrbrr_enforce** (rbrr_regime.sh)
   - Regex: `RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT` (NNNs), `RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX` (^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$)
   - Used by: rbrr-bad-timeout, rbrr-bad-runtime-prefix-* (3 cases)

4. **Custom format checks in zrbrd_enforce** (rbrd_regime.sh)
   - Regex: `RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX` (^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$), `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` (^[a-z][a-z0-9]*$)
   - Cross-check: joint length ≤ 30
   - Used by: rbrd-bad-cloud-prefix-*, rbrd-bad-moniker

5. **File existence checks** (buv_validation.sh `buv_dir_exists`, `buv_file_exists`)
   - Used by: rbrr-bad-vessel-dir, rbrr-bad-secrets-dir, burc-missing-station-file
   - Not poisonable via value tweak (requires missing file)

6. **Custom cross-variable enforcement** (rbrn_regime.sh `zrbrn_enforce`)
   - Entry port < uplink port min (conditional on ENTRY_MODE=rbnne_enabled)
   - IP subnet membership checks (ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP in ENCLAVE_BASE_IP/NETMASK)
   - Used by: rbrn-port-conflict

7. **Tabtarget refusal contracts** (rbw-dU empty-arg refusal)
   - Not value-based; argument contract
   - Used by: unmake-empty-arg-refusal (regime-smoke)

8. **Dockerfile hygiene (rbw-fhc)** & **Recipe validation (zrbld_spine_validate)**
   - Line-format parsing (Dockerfile FROM line), substitution-key coverage
   - Used by: dockerfile-hygiene negatives, recipe-validation negatives

---

### 4. ENROLLMENT-VALIDATION SPECIAL NOTES

**Scope**: Enrollment-validation fixtures exercise **BUV (Bash Validation Utility)** library directly, NOT regime files. They:
- Call `buv_regime_enroll`, `buv_group_enroll`, `buv_*_enroll` to define schema
- Call `buv_vet` to validate staged environment variables
- Use `RbtdrfSub` helper to run bare `buv_vet "SCOPE"` against inline values

**NOT regime-file-based**: Stages values inline as shell `export` statements, not in `.env` files. **Poison-tweak shape does NOT apply** — the tests already use direct value mutation. No file fabrication; pure value poisoning already in use.

**Rejection Gate**: Single gate — BUV's `zbuv_check_capture` type dispatcher and per-type validators.

---

### 5. CASES ASSERTING ON STDERR

**None explicitly assert stderr text content** across all fixtures. All negative cases assert **bare non-zero exit code** via `rbtdrf_run_probate`, `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg`, or `rbtdrf_run_ev` / `RbtdrfSub::fatal()`. Stdout/stderr are captured to trace files but not validated by test assertions.

Exception: `unmake-empty-arg-refusal` (regime-smoke) checks that combined stdout/stderr **contains** a pointer string (`RBTDGC_LIST_DEPOT`), but this is a substring presence check, not stderr-specific assertion.

---

## SUMMARY TABLE

| Fixture | Total | Pos | Neg | VALUE | FILE | OTHER | Key Gate(s) |
|---------|-------|-----|-----|-------|------|-------|-------------|
| enrollment-validation | 47 | 18 | 29 | 29 | 0 | 0 | BUV type checks |
| regime-validation | 48 | 15 | 33 | 19 | 13 | 1 | BUV + format regex + file checks |
| regime-smoke | 10 | 9 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | Tabtarget contracts |
| dockerfile-hygiene | 9 | 5 | 3 | 3 | 0 | 0 | rbw-fhc (FROM-line parser) |
| recipe-validation | 10 | 6 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 0 | zrbld_spine_validate |
| **TOTAL** | **124** | **53** | **70** | **55** | **13** | **2** |

---

## KEY FILES & LINE REFERENCES

- **Harness entry points**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`
  - `rbtdrf_run_probate_in()` (line 197): synthetic regime fabrication + probate dispatch
  - `rbtdrf_run_ev()` (line 142): enrollment-validation BUV direct invocation
  - Fixture registration (lines 2067–2262)

- **BUV validation core**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh`
  - `zbuv_check_capture()` (line 331): type dispatcher & per-type checks
  - `buv_scope_sentinel()` (line 613): unexpected-var guard
  - `buv_dir_exists()`, `buv_file_exists()` (lines 39, 34): file existence gates

- **Regime enforce gates**: 
  - RBRR: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbrr_regime.sh` (zrbrr_enforce)
  - RBRD: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbrd_regime.sh` (zrbrd_enforce)
  - RBRN: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbrn_regime.sh` (zrbrn_enforce)
  - BURS: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/buk/burs_regime.sh` (zburs_enforce)

---

## REDESIGN APPLICABILITY

**Poison-tweak shape (post-load BURE injection) applies to:**
- ✅ All 29 enrollment-validation negatives (already value-poisoned; no change)
- ✅ All 19 regime-validation VALUE-grain cases
- ✅ All 3 dockerfile-hygiene negatives
- ✅ All 4 recipe-validation negatives
- **Total: 55 / 70 negative cases convertible**

**NOT convertible (require file fabrication or other shapes):**
- ❌ 13 FILE-grain cases: missing directories (rbrr-bad-vessel-dir, rbrr-bad-secrets-dir), missing files (burc-missing-station-file), mode-dependent required fields (rbrv-no-bind-image, rbrv-partial-conjure), unset required fields in BUV vet
- ❌ 2 OTHER cases: scope sentinel catch (unexpected var), cross-variable port conflict, tabtarget argument refusal
- **Total: 15 / 70 require alternative approach**

content.text:
The census is in, and it both confirms your trust and surfaces one plan-changing collision. (Also: you wrote "heat Bb" — I'm assuming that was a slip for **₣Be**, since Bb is the acg-scrub heat whose rename pace gates Be.)

## Census results

70 negative cases across the five fast fixtures, boiling down to **6–8 distinct rejection gates** — comfortably inside the band's "roughly half a dozen codes":

| Fixture | Neg | Convertible to poison | Gate |
|---------|-----|----------------------|------|
| enrollment-validation | 29 | already is | `buv_vet` type checks — **one code covers all 29** |
| regime-validation | 33 | ~30 (see below) | BUV checks, per-regime `enforce` regexes, scope sentinel, file-existence |
| recipe-validation | 4 | n/a — honest as-is | `zrbld_spine_validate` |
| dockerfile-hygiene | 3 | n/a — honest as-is | `rbw-fhc` FROM-line parser |
| regime-smoke | 1 | n/a | empty-arg tabtarget contract |

Three findings that sharpen the plan:

1. **Enrollment-validation needs no conversion at all.** It never fabricates regime files — it already stages inline values against `buv_vet` directly. It's pure value-poisoning today; it only needs the band-code assertion migration. The original ₢BeAAD docket stands nearly as written.
2. **The "file-grain" residue mostly collapses if the poison seam supports set *and* unset.** The agent classified 13 cases file-grain, but most inject through a *variable* anyway: "bad vessel dir" poisons `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR` to a bogus path; "unexpected var" injects an extra `RBRR_BOGUS`; "missing required" is an unset; even the cross-variable port conflict is producible by moving one variable. With set/unset semantics in one BUK regime-load membrane, nearly all 33 regime negatives go in-universe and **all twelve `*_probate` side doors delete**.
3. **One honest caveat for the conversion pace:** "run the real verb against the real committed regime" assumes a valid baseline exists in-tree. It does for nameplates/vessels/repo regime; it does *not* for the operator-local regimes (station, payor, oauth, auth, depot) — which is probably why the probate fabrication existed in the first place. Those will need a staged valid baseline (fixture template, not Rust string-building). Discovery item for the conversion pace, not a plan-breaker.

## The collision your OAuth-fatal idea just exposed

If the fast runner exports the cloud-fatal **tweak** suite-wide, the slot is occupied for the whole run — and the regime-poison cases need that same slot per-case. The demolition condition we named two turns ago ("first fast case needing seam + constraint simultaneously") **fires on day one of this very plan**. So building cloud-fatal on the tweak slot would be building a known-broken membrane. The fix is cheap and aligns with the doctrine analysis anyway: the OAuth-fatal gate gets its **own dedicated flag variable** (BURE-family, shape-disciplined) from the start — it's standing policy, not a condition-forcing seam. Its doctrine-pure *proof case* still exists; the tweak slot stays free for the true seams (ensconce-stamp, regime-poison). Everything else about your idea survives intact: no zipper changes, no classification sweep, gate on actual credential use at the `rbgo`/`rba` chokepoint.

## The plan, assembled

Abandon ₢BHAAl in ₣BH (drop; repoint the memo's TRIAGED stamp — a ₢BHAAd straggler). Recreate in ₣Be:

1. ₢BeAAA band + `buc_die` membrane — unchanged, still first
2. ₢BeAAB numeric codegen — unchanged
3. *(new)* immure tweak eviction — resolve-only colophon split; band-independent, can run early
4. *(new)* graft tweak retirement — minimal repair (static value in graft-demo regime, delete both ends); installer verb stays deferred to the made-side heat
5. *(new)* cloud-fatal gate — dedicated flag, band code, fast runner exports it, proof case, convention documented (this is ₢BHAAl's intent, rebuilt)
6. *(reslate ₢BeAAC)* regime-validation conversion — poison seam (set/unset, one BUK membrane), real verbs, specific codes, probate doors deleted; opens with the baseline-availability census
7. ₢BeAAD enrollment-validation — code-assertion migration only, near-original docket
8. ₢BeAAE recipe + hygiene — near-original docket (their fabrication is honest: the artifact under test *is* a file)
9. ₢BeAAF prose — BCG band entry, extended to carry the tweak doctrine (or doctrine lands in BUS0; one prose pace can cover both homes)

Verdict: yes, this is a strong plan — every tweak in the final census is doctrine-compliant, the zippers are untouched, twelve test-only side doors die, and the ₣BH hazard closes with a few lines at a real chokepoint instead of a cross-cutting convention. The ₣Bb rename-pace gate still applies before any of it mounts.

Ready to draft the reslates and new dockets for ₣Be plus the ₢BHAAl drop when you are.

message.content: Hold on, I am reticent to add a new mechanism if instead of running the regime negative cases inside of fast (which would not work given both the cloud denial tweak and the regime variable alteration tweak simultaneous), we could have a whole separate fixture for the regime validation that is run in a rarer circumstance, but still regularly run.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — and I think this is the better resolution, with one reframing and one happy discovery from the census that makes the move smaller than you might fear.

## The reframing: it buys you an invariant, not just a workaround

Theurge composes env per invocation, so technically the runner *could* yield the guard on tweaked invocations — but that's an invisible coupling (setting a tweak silently disables the guard), exactly the kind of non-load-bearing cleverness worth refusing. Your separation instead produces a crisp, teachable suite-level rule:

> **Fast cases use no tweaks. In the fast tier, the tweak slot belongs to the credless guard.**

That's stronger than collision-avoidance — it's a statement about what fast *is*. And it composes with your doctrine naturally: tweaks force rare conditions; condition-forcing cases are by nature not smoke-tier. Any future case that wants a seam has self-identified as not belonging in fast. No new mechanism, no dedicated flag, no BURE growth.

## The census makes the move surgical

Almost nothing actually has to leave fast, because almost nothing else needs the tweak slot:

- **Enrollment-validation's 29 negatives stay** — they stage values as inline exports against `buv_vet` directly, no tweak involved, no cloud reach. Untouched.
- **Recipe-validation and dockerfile-hygiene negatives stay** — synthetic file arguments, no tweak.
- **Regime-validation's ~15 positive/baseline cases stay** — validating real committed regimes is guarded, credless, tweak-free.
- **Only the new in-universe poison negatives move** — the ~33 conversions form the new fixture (call it regime-negatives or similar), the sole tweak consumer in the local-only tier.

So the split is clean: fast keeps everything it has today minus nothing, gains the guard; the new fixture is born outside fast rather than evicted from it.

## Where "rarer but regularly run" lands

The fixture is dependency-free (no creds, no container runtime), so it can ride *every* suite above fast at zero precondition cost. I'd enroll it in **service, crucible, and complete** — in practice that's most working days, while bare fast stays pure. Two compensations for the one real cost (bare fast no longer proves the regime gates' negatives):

- The single-fixture tabtarget (`tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh regime-negatives`) is the targeted tool, and the CLAUDE.md test table's "Regime/validation changes → `fast`" line updates to "→ `fast` + the regime-negatives fixture." That's the same muscle as the existing per-tier guidance.
- The positives staying in fast means a *gross* regime breakage (loader dies, baseline invalid) still fails fast immediately; what waits for the next service/crucible run is only "a gate stopped rejecting" — a real but slower-burning regression class, which is acceptable for a rarer-but-regular cadence.

## Plan delta

Unchanged: band paces first, immure eviction, graft retirement, enrollment/recipe/hygiene migrations near-original, prose pace. Changes:

- **Cloud guard pace**: rides the tweak slot after all (no dedicated flag); fast runner sets it on every invocation; the fast-uses-no-tweaks invariant gets stated alongside the doctrine in the prose pace.
- **Regime conversion pace**: builds the poison seam *and* the new non-fast fixture together; probate doors still delete; suite enrollment to service/crucible/complete; CLAUDE.md table line updates.
- The single-slot demolition condition still gets recorded once, now with a cleaner trigger: if a *non-fast* case ever needs two simultaneous tweaks, that's the day the dedicated variable gets minted.

This is a tighter plan than the last iteration — one mechanism, one invariant, twelve side doors deleted, and the tweak census stays doctrine-pure. Ready to draft when you are.
